title: Gyárfás-Sumner Conjecture (Conjecture 1.1)
statement_text: For every forest $H$, there is a function $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\omega(G))$ for every $H$-free graph $G$.
context_text: The Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture asserts that the class of $H$-free graphs is $\chi$-bounded for every forest $H$. It remains open in general and has been proved only for restricted families of trees. Any forest satisfying Esperet's polynomial strengthening (Conjecture 1.2) automatically satisfies this conjecture.
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture for all star-forests $H$ as an implicit consequence of the main theorem establishing polynomial $\chi$-binding functions for those forests.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. II. Excluding a star-forest

title: Esperet's Conjecture (Conjecture 1.2)
statement_text: There is a polynomial $f_H$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f_H(\omega(G))$ for every $H$-free graph $G$.
context_text: Esperet conjectured that every $\chi$-bounded class is polynomially $\chi$-bounded. For forest exclusion specifically, the conjecture was previously verified only for disjoint unions of $K_2$, graphs obtained from a star by subdividing one edge once, and their induced subgraphs. In particular, it was not known for $H = P_5$.
paper_contribution: Proves Esperet's conjecture for all star-forests $H$ (forests in which every component is a star), via the main theorem: if $H'$ satisfies the conjecture and $H$ is the disjoint union of $H'$ and a star, then $H$ also satisfies the conjecture.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. II. Excluding a star-forest

title: Informal remark on completeness of known forests satisfying Esperet's conjecture
statement_text: As far as we know (although it seems unlikely), [the forests described in results 1.4–1.6 and their induced subgraphs] might be all the forests that satisfy [Esperet's conjecture 1.2].
context_text: After establishing their main results on star-forests and double stars, the authors pause to note the state of knowledge: the only forests known to satisfy Esperet's polynomial conjecture are those built from disjoint unions of stars, double stars, and $K_2$. They remark that these may be all such forests, though they consider this unlikely.
notes: Authors signal explicit scepticism ('although it seems unlikely'), so this is a note on the limits of current knowledge rather than a firm conjecture.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. II. Excluding a star-forest
